message:
chore(beads): sync issues.jsonl with three new in_progress transitions

Routine drift sync absorbing the parallel-agent transitions emitted
since the last frankenterm beads commit:

- ft-6qkx1 ("storage.rs Phase 2 — extract pub structs") moved
  open → in_progress. This is the natural successor to Phase 1a /
  Phase 3 storage.rs extraction work that just landed in 35fa605c5
  and 81fca11c5.
- ft-l6sq5 ("[mcp-v1-002] register wa.workflow_status as MCP tool")
  moved open → in_progress.
- ft-3p7re ("[codebase-audit][workflows] no overall workflow
  deadline") moved open → in_progress.

Pure tracking sync — no source code, test code, or configuration
changes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -54,7 +54,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-3l5bu","title":"[MEDIUM] RateLimiter.pane_counts HashMap leaks entries for every (pane_id, action) pair that is ever checked","description":"# Defect

## File + line

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2103` — `RateLimiter::check`.

## Concrete failure path

Every `check(action, Some(pane_id))` call executes

    let timestamps = self.pane_counts.entry((pane_id, action)).or_default();

which **always** inserts a `Vec<Instant>` under `(pane_id, action)` if it doesn't exist, before any limit logic runs. Same story for `global_counts` keyed by `ActionKind`.

After a pane is destroyed, nothing removes its entries. `prune_old(timestamps, window_start)` removes expired timestamps from the Vec but leaves the Vec itself and the HashMap entry in place. Over the lifetime of a long-running `PolicyEngine`, the HashMap grows monotonically:

* Bound per pane: at most one entry per `ActionKind` variant (finite enum).
* Bound across panes: `unique(pane_id) × |ActionKind|`.

On a 200+-pane swarm churning through ~1000 panes/day over 90 days, that's ~90k × |ActionKind| entries at ~16 bytes key + baseline Vec — not a crash, but a monotonic leak tracked nowhere. **Same class as ft-l6v1r** (`pane_activity_tracker`) and the `BackpressureMetrics::dropped_by_pane` leak I fixed earlier (f2390da9).

## Worse-case path: pane_id reuse

The ft-l6v1r commit observed that wezterm can reuse pane_ids. If a reused pane_id hashes to an old (pane_id, action) entry whose Vec still contains stale timestamps, the new pane inherits the dead pane's rate-limit budget — either under-counted (if the prune pass has already emptied the Vec) or over-counted (if timestamps haven't aged out yet). The current `check` path prunes based on `window_start` which is relative to `Instant::now()`, so stale timestamps from a destroyed pane will age out correctly — but entries without any timestamps will still mis-attribute the **first** rate-limited hit back to the old pane's history in any diagnostic that reads `pane_counts` directly.

## Fix scope

1. `RateLimiter::cleanup_pane(pane_id) -> usize` — remove every `(pane_id, _)` entry from `pane_counts`, return the number removed for telemetry / debug_assert. Cheap: one full iteration.
2. Unit test pinning: entries present before, zero after, unrelated panes untouched, idempotent on panes never inserted.
3. Wiring into the PaneDestroyed teardown path is a separate follow-up bead — `PolicyEngine` is not currently plumbed through `ObservationRuntime::spawn_discovery_task` or `handle_native_event`, and that plumbing is a broader cross-boundary change that belongs on its own bead.

Single-file scope: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs`.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.564595Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:54:31.244870Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:54:31.244127Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-3mf5z","title":"[test][cleanup] post-runtime_compat-removal vestigial e2e checks/script names","description":"Audit-mode finding (review of swarm session 2026-04-26 commits 4ccf4175 / 4afa65db / 4728dd81). After ft-y378j.4 removed the runtime_compat module alias, two e2e shell artifacts still encode the pre-removal world:

1. tests/e2e/test_ft_2toy3_feature_gated_migration.sh:67-86 (S2 check):
   - 'S2: Feature-gated modules use runtime_compat...' — checks for runtime_compat refs in web.rs/mcp.rs/distributed.rs.
   - PASS branch (RC_USAGE >= 1) is now unreachable (alias removed).
   - else branch fires unconditionally with message 'OK (0 refs — sync-only modules may not need runtime_compat)' which misrepresents reality (the alias was REMOVED, not 'modules don't need it').
   - emit_log key 'runtime_compat_usage' / value 'sync_only' may be consumed by CI parsers — changing keys is risky.
   - Action: either delete S2 entirely (the migration it verified is complete), or repurpose to verify NO runtime_compat refs remain (positive guard); the existing scripts/check_runtime_compat_residuals.sh already does the latter, so deletion is cleaner.

2. tests/e2e/test_ft_e34d9_10_2_3_runtime_compat_contraction.sh:
   - File NAME contains runtime_compat. Internal validate_runtime_compat_helper_callsites function checks that production code does not import compat shims.
   - Now a tautology since the module doesn't exist.
   - Action: rename file to test_ft_e34d9_10_2_3_runtime_async_contraction.sh and update CI matrix references; or delete if subsumed by check_runtime_compat_residuals.sh.

3. test_ft_e34d9_10_5_4_runtime_surface_guard.sh:351-356 'runtime_compat helper shims' scenario — same vestigial-check pattern.

Both findings are post-removal cosmetic drift; the workspace builds and tests pass. Cleanup in a focused PR after deciding the deletion-vs-repurpose policy.

Reference: swarm review 2026-04-26 (orchestrator audit pass).

(1) test_ft_2toy3_feature_gated_migration.sh:67-86 S2 check repurposed from vestigial 'use runtime_compat?' to positive guard 'free of runtime_compat alias?' — emit_log key updated to runtime_compat_alias_removed/clean|residual. (2) test_ft_e34d9_10_2_3_runtime_compat_contraction.sh git-mv'd to test_ft_e34d9_10_2_3_runtime_async_contraction.sh; internal validate_runtime_compat_helper_callsites → validate_runtime_async_helper_callsites; log file pattern, scenario_id rch_init arg, and final echo all updated; doc reference in docs/asupersync-runtime-compat-surface-ft-e34d9.10.2.3.md updated (3 callsites). (3) test_ft_e34d9_10_5_4_runtime_surface_guard.sh:351 'Scenario 4: runtime_compat helper shims' → 'runtime_async helper shims'; helper function and record_result key renamed to match. Top docstring line 8 also updated. Verification: bash -n clean on all 3; #1 ran end-to-end (8 passed / 0 failed). Historical comments explaining the rename history kept in place.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cleanup","runtime","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-3mo4e","title":"mux dispatch conformance covers chunk-boundary matrix","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:00:45.678473Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:05:18.708899Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:05:18.708657Z","close_reason":"Landed in 4f9138e6","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-3p7re","title":"[codebase-audit][workflows] no overall workflow deadline — step_timeout_ms only; pathological retry loops can pin a pane forever","description":"## Domain gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:218-237`:

```rust
pub struct WorkflowRunnerConfig {
    pub max_concurrent: usize,         // 3
    pub step_timeout_ms: u64,          // 30_000
    pub retry_backoff_multiplier: f64, // 2.0
    pub max_retries_per_step: usize,   // 3
}
```

The runner caps **per-step** timeout (30s) and **per-step**
retry count (3). It does NOT cap **total workflow runtime**.

Concrete arithmetic: a workflow with N steps, M retries each,
exponential backoff:

- 10 steps × 3 retries × (1 + 2 + 4)s = 210s minimum runtime
- + 30s timeout per step × 10 = 300s additional ceiling
- = up to **510 seconds** for a "well-behaved" workflow

For a pathological workflow with longer step counts, custom
retry policies, or `WaitCondition::External` waits (per ft-ao9k9)
that hold for their full timeout: **the workflow can pin a pane
indefinitely**.

The pane lock is held for the workflow's full duration (per
runner.rs:367, lock_manager.try_acquire_with_limit). A pinned
pane means no other workflow can drive that pane.

## Why this is a real defect

Concrete failure scenarios:

1. **Wedged WaitCondition::External**: Per ft-ao9k9 fix, an
   external signal that NEVER fires hits the timeout. If the
   timeout is configured to a large value (e.g., 1h for a
   long-running mission), the pane is locked for an hour.

2. **Pathological retry loops.** A `Send` action whose
   verification step (the wait_for) consistently times out
   triggers retries; each retry burns the step budget; the
   workflow never makes forward progress but holds the lock.

3. **Operator can't cancel.** There's no documented `ft
   workflow stop <id>` that GUARANTEES a stuck workflow yields
   the lock within bounded time. A canceled Cx must propagate
   through every layer (per the modes-of-reasoning ft-k3y0u
   bead's drop-ordering work) — uncertain today.

The skill's domain audit for "workflows" surfaces this exactly:
it's the integration of step-timeout + retry-count + wait-
duration into a TOTAL runtime that's missing. Each component
has a bound; the composition doesn't.

## Suggested approach

1. **Add `total_timeout_ms` to `WorkflowRunnerConfig`**:

   ```rust
   pub struct WorkflowRunnerConfig {
       ...
       /// Maximum total runtime for any single workflow execution.
       /// Workflows running past this deadline are forcibly canceled
       /// AND the pane lock is released.
       pub total_timeout_ms: u64,   // suggested default: 600_000 (10 min)
   }
   ```

2. **Wire deadline propagation** through `execute_steps`:
   - Compute `deadline = start_time + total_timeout_ms` at
     workflow entry.
   - Each step gets a `step_deadline = min(step_timeout, deadline - now)`.
   - When deadline passes, return `WorkflowExecutionResult::TimedOut {
       execution_id, last_step, elapsed_ms
     }` and release the pane lock.

3. **Operator override**: per-workflow `total_timeout_ms` in the
   `Workflow` trait (each workflow declares its expected max
   runtime; runner enforces).

4. **Telemetry**: counter for
   `workflow.deadline_exceeded`, histogram for
   `workflow.execution_total_ms` so an operator can see
   pathological-runtime tail in `ft doctor`.

5. **Test**: `tests/workflow_total_deadline.rs` with a
   pathological workflow (always-timing-out external wait);
   assert deadline fires AND pane lock is released within the
   deadline + safety margin.

## Out of scope

- The `PaneLockGuard` refactor (separate bead ft-qkd2f covers
  the lock-leak invariant; this bead is about WHEN the
  guarded execution should be canceled).
- Workflow priority / preemption (different design surface).

## Acceptance criteria

- `WorkflowRunnerConfig::total_timeout_ms` field exists.
- The execution loop respects the deadline + emits a clean
  TimedOut result.
- The pane lock is released on deadline expiry.
- Regression test asserts a pathological 10-step workflow
  hits the deadline within tolerance and yields the lock.
- `ft doctor` exposes the `workflow.deadline_exceeded` counter.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:218-237`
  (the config struct missing total_timeout)
- ft-ao9k9 (sibling: ExternalSignalRegistry wait — this is
  exactly the un-firing-external pathology)
- ft-qkd2f (sibling: PaneLockGuard refactor — same workflow
  layer, different invariant)
- ft-j0ufc (sibling: workflow privilege amplification —
  source-pane-trust separate from this deadline issue)

Filed by cc_1 codebase audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_1","created_at":"2026-04-28T10:28:50.792103Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T21:19:51.004580Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-3r0n4","title":"[profiling] wa.state at fleet scale: 200+ pane envelope serialization unbenched (mcp_response covers manifest only)","description":"## Hypothesis

`ft robot state` / `wa.state` is the highest-frequency robot call
in a swarm — every operator tick (every ~4 minutes per the
operator runbook) calls it once for fleet observability, plus
agent-driven calls.

The README sells frankenterm as "swarm-native" specifically for
**200+ concurrent AI coding agents**. The per-call cost of
`wa.state` at fleet scale (200 panes) directly drives the
operator-loop tail latency budget.

Existing benches:

- `benches/mcp_response.rs` — covers the **manifest list**
  serialization (tools[]) at varying tool counts, NOT the per-
  call response payload at varying pane counts.
- `benches/dashboard_aggregation.rs` — covers dashboard-side
  aggregation, not the robot-mode envelope production.

`grep -lE "wa.state|RobotStateResponse|render_robot_state"
benches/*.rs` returns zero hits.

## Why a profile + bench would deliver real perf gain

`wa.state` builds an envelope of `Vec<PaneInfo>` from the live
mux; serializes; redacts; renders TOON or JSON. At 200 panes:

- **Mux query cost** — how many RPCs to wezterm? One bulk vs
  one-per-pane?
- **Redaction cost** — secret patterns run against every pane's
  metadata; aho-corasick matcher passes per pane; bounded by
  pattern-pack size, but unmeasured.
- **Serialization cost** — JSON vs TOON delta at 200 panes.
- **Memory** — peak RSS during the envelope build; if the
  builder allocates a temporary `Vec<PaneInfo>` plus a JSON
  string + a TOON string, that's 3 copies of the same data.
- **Allocation hot path** — how many heap allocs for a 200-pane
  envelope? Each pane has nested HashMap<String, Value> in
  `extra` — those are small allocs that dominate.

Per the skill's scaling-law discipline: capture p95 ratio across
1, 10, 50, 100, 200 panes — if the curve is super-linear, there's
real attribution work to do.

## Suggested instrumentation + bench

1. **Bench**: `crates/frankenterm-core/benches/robot_state_at_scale.rs`
   - Construct a synthetic mux with 1, 10, 50, 100, 200, 500
     panes (deterministic PaneInfo generators).
   - Drive the same code path `wa.state` calls (likely
     `mcp_helpers::handle_state` or equivalent).
   - Measure: p50/p95/p99/p99.9 ms, ops/sec, peak RSS, alloc
     count per call (via dhat).
   - Output formats: JSON + TOON, both measured.

2. **Profile flame** at 200 panes for 30 seconds with samply.

3. **Hot-path attribution** — split:
   - Mux RPC (fastest)
   - PaneInfo construction
   - Serialization (JSON vs TOON — feeds into ft-0zoq3)
   - Redaction sweep

4. **Differential**: compare 200-pane wa.state to 200-pane
   `ft list` plain-text rendering (no MCP envelope) to isolate
   the envelope-build cost from the data-fetch cost.

## Hypothesis ledger entries

```
serialization-not-mux-dominates : pending — flame shows
                                   serde_json::to_string >>
                                   mux::list_panes
extra-hashmap-allocs-dominate   : pending — dhat shows
                                   HashMap<String, Value> per
                                   PaneInfo dominates count
toon-2x-slower-than-json-here   : pending — links to ft-0zoq3
                                   evidence
super-linear-scaling            : pending — p95 ratio at 200/1
                                   exceeds 250
peak-rss-3x-payload-size        : pending — RSS during build
                                   exceeds final envelope size
                                   by ≥3×
```

## Out of scope

- Mux RPC plumbing (`mux_client_ops.rs`,
  `mux_pool_scaling.rs` already cover it).
- The TOON encoder itself (sibling: ft-0zoq3 — TOON encoder
  bench).
- The dashboard rendering (`dashboard_aggregation.rs`).

## Acceptance criteria

- `benches/robot_state_at_scale.rs` exists, exercises 6 pane-
  count points × 2 output formats.
- Hotspot table at 200 panes attributes cost across the four
  hot-path stages.
- Hypothesis ledger entries marked with evidence paths.
- Profile artifact under
  `tests/artifacts/perf/wa-state-fleet-<run-id>/` with
  fingerprint.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_helpers.rs` (likely
  `wa.state` handler)
- README §Tiered Scrollback ("designed for 200+ pane fleets")
- `docs/operator-runbook.md` §3 ("4-minute steady-state tick"
  cadence)
- Sibling bead: ft-0zoq3 (TOON encoder bench feeds the format
  comparison side)

Filed by cc_1 profiling audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_1","created_at":"2026-04-28T09:46:42.635866Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T11:11:40.110370Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T11:11:40.110148Z","close_reason":"wa.state fleet-scale bench + perf ledger shipped at commit b76c662d7. crates/frankenterm-core/benches/wa_state_fleet.rs (267 lines) measures 4 pipelines (construct_only, construct_redact, full_envelope_json, full_envelope_toon) × 3 pane counts (10, 50, 200) = 24 measurement points. Differentiated from sibling toon_encoding (ft-0zoq3, encoder CPU only) by including the redactor sweep + envelope wrapping cost split — subtraction of group medians attributes cost across redactor / json-serialize / toon-serialize. Half the synthetic panes carry an OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-fake… fragment in cwd so the redactor's regex actually fires. docs/perf-ledger/wa-state-fleet.md (84 lines) lists 5 pre-measurement hypotheses (H1: linear construct scaling, H2: redactor dominates at 200p, H3: TOON 1.5-2x slower than JSON, H4: TOON 30-40% smaller, H5: super-linear scaling 50→200 from Value's BTreeMap work) and a CI-populated measured-numbers table. Per the profiling skill's hand-off contract, this bead stops at the hotspot table; optimization (if hypotheses supported) becomes a follow-on bead. Verified: cargo check -p frankenterm-core --bench wa_state_fleet exits 0 in 1m 41s cold.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-3se13","title":"[security-audit] policy decision log captures attacker-controlled text via PolicyInput.text_summary — secrets-in-audit leak","description":"## Concrete attack

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4900` (the canonical
authorize entry):

```rust
pub fn authorize(&mut self, input: &PolicyInput) -> PolicyDecision {
    let decision = self.evaluate_authorization(input, None, None, true);
    self.record_to_decision_log(input, &decision);   // ← logs INPUT
    decision
}
```

`PolicyInput` carries a `text_summary` field that callers populate
with the action's payload. Per `mcp_tools.rs:1524`:

```rust
let summary = format!("wa.get_text pane_id={}", params.pane_id);
let mut input = mcp_get_text_policy_input(params.pane_id, ..., &summary);
```

That particular case is metadata-only. But other call sites pass
the actual TEXT being sent:

```rust
// mcp_tools.rs send-text path
input = input.with_text_summary(format!("send_text {} bytes", text.len()))
   .with_command_text(text.clone());   // ← whole user-typed text
```

`with_command_text(text.clone())` puts the FULL text into the
policy input. `record_to_decision_log` then writes it to the
decision log without going through `redact_secrets()`.

If a user (or the SOURCE agent) types a secret into a pane via
`ft robot send-text` or MCP `wa.send_text`, that secret is:

1. **NOT redacted on the way IN** — that's by design (you're
   intentionally sending it).
2. **Stored in the decision log unredacted** — because the
   decision log captures the ENTIRE PolicyInput including
   command_text, and the redactor isn't applied to the audit
   record before persistence.

Result: the audit/forensics surface accumulates secrets in
plaintext over time. An attacker with read access to the audit
log (legitimate operator? compromised support engineer?
forensic-export consumer?) gets every secret ever sent.

## Why this is exploitable

Per the skill's `⊕ Creative-Transposition` operator
(disgruntled-insider persona): an ex-employee with offline
copies of the audit log can mine years of operator typing for
credentials. Per Axiom 7 (recovery path is shadow codebase),
the audit log is a recovery surface that should re-enforce the
same redaction invariants as the live read surfaces — and
doesn't.

The `redactor.rs` test surface specifically covers
`AuditActionRecord::redact_fields` (storage.rs:2966) — which
proves the project's authors KNEW the audit surface needed
redaction. But the redactor is applied DOWNSTREAM at storage
write time on a different code path; the decision log captured
inside the policy engine doesn't go through that same redactor.

## Reproduction (concrete)

```bash
# Send a fake secret via MCP send-text (any matching prefix)
echo '{"pane_id":0,"text":"export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-fake1234567890abcdef..."}' | \
  cargo run -p frankenterm -- mcp serve --stdio

# Inspect the decision log
sqlite3 ~/.local/share/ft/db.sqlite \
  "SELECT json_extract(input, '$.command_text') FROM policy_decision_log
   ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1"
# → "export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-fake1234567890abcdef..."   (UNREDACTED)
```

## Suggested fix

1. **Apply redactor at decision-log write time.** Modify
   `record_to_decision_log` to redact `text_summary` and
   `command_text` before persisting:

   ```rust
   fn record_to_decision_log(&mut self, input: &PolicyInput, decision: &PolicyDecision) {
       let mut redacted_input = input.clone();
       if let Some(t) = redacted_input.text_summary.as_ref() {
           redacted_input.text_summary = Some(self.redact_secrets(t));
       }
       if let Some(c) = redacted_input.command_text.as_ref() {
           redacted_input.command_text = Some(self.redact_secrets(c));
       }
       // existing persistence logic, with redacted_input
   }
   ```

2. **Add a regression test**
   `tests/policy_decision_log_redacts_secrets.rs` that:
   - Constructs a `PolicyInput` with `command_text` containing
     each provider token family (xai-, sk-ant-, gh*_, AKIA, etc.).
   - Calls `authorize`.
   - Reads back from the decision-log table.
   - Asserts every column with text content is redacted.

3. **Document the contract** in
   `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs` doc comment: "the
   decision log redacts secrets before persistence; callers
   must NOT assume audit retains plaintext."

## Out of scope

- Field-level encryption at rest (orthogonal — separate bead if
  enterprises need it).
- Forensic-export redaction (`forensic_export.rs` has its own
  redaction pass — verify alignment in a sibling bead if
  needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `record_to_decision_log` applies the redactor to text fields
  before persistence.
- Regression test asserts the matrix above (≥7 provider
  families) is fully redacted in the decision-log table.
- README §Safety Guarantees gains an explicit "audit logs are
  redacted in addition to live read surfaces."

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4900-4906` (the
  authorize → record_to_decision_log call)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:5874` (engine.redact_secrets
  exists; not wired to the decision log)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:2966`
  (`AuditActionRecord::redact_fields` — the partner surface that
  IS wired)
- README §Safety Guarantees
- Skill: security-audit-for-saas Axiom 7 (recovery path is
  shadow codebase) + KEY-MANAGEMENT.md (PII never in error
  messages, logs, or stack traces)

Filed by cc_1 security audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc_1","created_at":"2026-04-28T10:08:46.417273Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T20:29:59.427902Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T20:29:59.427684Z","close_reason":"Shipped (commit d0ce8874e). Added LazyLock<Redactor> at policy_decision_log module level; PolicyDecisionLog::record() now runs reason.map(|s| REDACTOR.redact(&s)) BEFORE building the entry — the redaction invariant lives at the storage boundary, so a future caller cannot route around the scrub without rewriting the log itself. rule_id is left untouched (stable static slug; redacting risks false positives on dotted paths). The ft-3xek9 redactor work earlier this session means this scrub covers all 18+ provider patterns. 7 new fixture tests in policy_decision_log::tests pin redaction of OpenAI/Anthropic/xAI/GitHub PAT secrets in reason field, no-false-positive on benign reasons, redaction visible through entries() iterator (closes export-surface loop), and None-stays-None. All 24 policy_decision_log:: tests pass under --no-default-features (was 17, +7 ft-3se13). Defense-in-depth pattern: if a future PolicyDecision constructor or DSL rule echoes attacker-supplied text (PolicyInput.text_summary, matched_pattern fragment, capability denial detail) into the reason, the secret bytes are stripped at the storage boundary before persisting through telemetry snapshots / audit dumps / JSON export.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-3tvvt","title":"[modes-of-reasoning][async-vs-sync] storage.rs has 6+ block_on calls — library secretly owns the runtime instead of borrowing from it","description":"## Perspective: async vs sync boundary

A library that calls `block_on(...)` is making a unilateral
decision about runtime ownership: *"I will start my own executor
to drive this Future to completion"*. That is binary behavior
disguised as library code.

Concrete evidence in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`:

- Line 12215: `crate::runtime_async::CompatRuntime::block_on(&runtime, rx.recv(&recv_cx)).ok();`
- Line 23543: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 27947: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 29831: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 30090: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 30460: `let verification_results: Vec<(u64, Vec<u64>)> = rt.block_on(async { ... });`

Six `block_on` call sites inside a library file. The README's
"Platform Model" section says the runtime model is
"asupersync-native in the product-facing APIs: structured,
cancel-correct, and centered on Cx-aware orchestration" — but
`block_on` inside library code is the OPPOSITE of cancel-correct
and Cx-aware: it captures the calling thread, ignores any
ambient `Cx`, and converts a structured async failure into a
sync deadlock if the caller is already on a runtime.

## Why this is a real architectural concern

Two cascading risks:

1. **Deadlock if a downstream crate is already on a runtime.**
   `frankenterm-gui` and `frankenterm-mux-server` are async
   binaries. If either calls a storage path that internally
   `block_on`s while ALREADY on the gui's wgpu/winit dispatcher
   thread, the runtime-within-runtime nesting deadlocks. Tokio
   panics on this; asupersync's behavior is documented in
   AGENTS.md but the risk is real.

2. **Cx cancellation is bypassed.** A `block_on` ignores the
   ambient cancellation context. If the caller's Cx is
   canceled, the storage operation continues to completion.
   This contradicts MEMORY.md's `asupersync-midflight-cancel-gap`
   note exactly: storage paths can block past a cancel signal.

The README explicitly markets cancel-correctness as a load-bearing
contract. Library code that internally `block_on`s breaks that
contract for any caller path it touches.

## Suggested approach

1. **Inventory the six call sites.** For each, classify:
   - Is this called from sync code only? (then OK at the binary
     boundary, but should still be moved OUT of the library)
   - Is this called from async code? (then it's a deadlock waiting
     to happen; convert to `.await`)
   - Is this in a `Drop` impl? (acceptable per
     `cancellation_safe_channel` patterns, but document why)

2. **Forbid new `block_on` inside `frankenterm-core`** via a
   `#[clippy::disallowed_methods]` lint or a custom check in
   `scripts/check_no_runtime_regression.sh` (which already
   guards similar patterns).

3. **For the async-call-site cases**: convert to `pub async fn`
   and let the binary decide.

4. **For the sync-call-site cases**: extract the sync wrapper
   to the binary crate (`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs` or a
   new `crates/frankenterm-cli-storage` shim).

5. **Add a regression test** that asserts `frankenterm-core`
   compiles + tests pass with `block_on` mechanically removed,
   so future drift surfaces in CI.

## Out of scope

- `runtime_async::CompatRuntime::block_on` itself (it is the
  documented compat shim per AGENTS.md / ft-xbnl0; this bead
  removes its callers from library code, not the shim).
- Drop-impl cleanup paths that legitimately need to drain a
  channel synchronously (those are the documented `cancellation_safe_channel`
  pattern; flag them with a comment instead of removing).

## Acceptance criteria

- Zero `block_on(` matches under `crates/frankenterm-core/src/`
  except in `runtime_async.rs`, `runtime.rs`, and any documented
  exception list.
- `scripts/check_no_runtime_regression.sh` is extended to gate
  this in CI.
- The six current call sites are individually addressed in
  follow-on PRs (one per site, with tests proving the new
  async-or-binary placement preserves behavior).

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:12215, 23543, 27947, 29831, 30090, 30460`
- README §Platform Model (cancel-correctness contract)
- MEMORY.md `asupersync-midflight-cancel-gap` (the related
  pre-cancel/mid-flight asymmetry)
- `scripts/check_no_runtime_regression.sh` (existing CI guard
  pattern to extend)
- ft-xbnl0 epic (the broader runtime-collapse tracking)

 {"id":"ft-6mmyp","title":"[review][MEDIUM] ft-x86z2 gate returns MCP_ERR_POLICY on Deny/RequireApproval without recording an audit action","description":"Proactive policy.rs audit round 20. a4b7c8c3's mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation helper at mcp_tools.rs:177-206 returns an MCP_ERR_POLICY error envelope on Deny or RequireApproval and returns early — WITHOUT calling storage.record_audit_action_redacted or any equivalent. Effect: a policy deny on wa.tx_run, wa.tx_rollback, wa.mission_pause, wa.mission_resume, or wa.mission_abort leaves NO audit trail. Operators reviewing a suspicious activity burst cannot correlate MCP client attempts against the audit log — the denied attempts are not in it. Compare wa.events_annotate at mcp_tools.rs:4330-4352 which writes an AuditActionRecord AFTER the mutation. Successful mutations are audited; denied mutations are not. That asymmetry defeats the purpose of a security audit log. Fix: extend mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation to take the storage handle and record an AuditActionRecord with result=denied and decision_reason=policy_reason(decision) before returning. Or write a parallel log stream dedicated to policy-denied attempts. Related ft-eu0no HIGH (rate limiter ineffective) means the missing audit is especially bad because denies that should happen via rate limiter also never happen. Severity MEDIUM: security-observability gap, not a direct exploit, but blocks forensics on any real attack. Audit round 20, 2026-04-23. Tag: review, security, mcp, audit, policy.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:27:33.470763Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:32:15.407352Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:32:15.407068Z","close_reason":"MVP shipped in 02988c16: mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation now emits structured tracing::warn! on every Deny/RequireApproval under target ft::security::policy with tool/command/decision/rule_id/reason fields. Closes the zero-observability gap — operators running RUST_LOG=ft::security::policy=warn (or a SIEM tailing journald for that target) see every denied MCP mutation in real time with enough structure to pivot on the rule_id.

Full record_audit_action_redacted write (the bead's ideal) requires either an async helper or sync-path-opens-storage machinery the current gate doesn't have — filed ft-h90rh with a concrete GateOutcome-enum refactor path so callers (which already live inside runtime.block_on scopes) can own the audit write themselves.

Note on user framing: the task summary mentioned 'more specific error code like FT-MCP-POLICY-DENIED' as an alternative interpretation, but the bead body actually asks for audit observability on denies. Stayed with the bead's intent. Error code / hint UX (if desired) can be a separate bead against the same call site.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-6mxfs","title":"[mor:security-engineering] ws_dir = root.join('.ft','patterns') is not canonicalized before discovery","description":"# Observation

In `load_packs_from_config`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:949):

    let ws_dir = root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns");
    let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&ws_dir)?;

ft-fbee5 (just closed) fixed the case where a symlink INSIDE
`.ft/patterns/` escapes the sandbox. But the parent path is itself
just a join, no canonicalize. A hostile cloned repo can ship:

    .ft/patterns -> /etc

(a symlink at the directory level.) `ws_dir` canonicalizes to
`/etc`, `discover_packs_from_dir(/etc)` iterates there, and the
per-file sandbox check passes because it canonicalizes with root =
`/etc`. The symlink escape is now one level up.

Similar attack surface for `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` if
that ever returns a joined path the caller doesn't canonicalize.

# Question

Should `load_packs_from_config` canonicalize `ws_dir` and verify the
result resolves *inside* `root_path.canonicalize()` before calling
`discover_packs_from_dir`? That closes the parent-level symlink
vector without needing to change `discover_packs_from_dir`'s
contract.

Stretch: is there a case for centralizing this as a
`sandbox_resolve_dir(dir, root)` helper and using it everywhere the
pack/workflow/policy loaders join a workspace-relative path? The
pattern is probably not unique to pack loading.

# Trust boundary

The repo is the attacker; the user's home directory is the victim.
Everything a hostile repo can make `read_to_string` see is a
potential data-exfil primitive. Treat repo-relative path
construction as crossing a trust boundary, and canonicalize at the
boundary crossing, not at the leaf.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:09.320199Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.174562Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305207Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0239fbd6 — added sandbox_resolve_dir helper, routed load_packs_from_config's ws_dir through it with 4 regression tests (missing, inside-root, toplevel-symlink-escape, end-to-end).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","sandboxing","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-6piwt","title":"[P0 BLOCKER] frankenterm-core lib has 61 compile errors under --no-default-features — blocks all --no-default-features test/bench runs","description":"## Symptom
`cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features` fails with 61 errors. Panes 3 and 4 in the swarm both hit this while trying to verify their respective commits.

## Representative error
```
error[E0599]: no method named `record_event_with_cx` found for this struct
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:6157
  help: there is a method `record_event` with a similar name

error[E0599]: no method named `get_text_with_cx` found for struct `std::sync::Arc<(dyn WeztermInterface + 'static)>`
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:3272:43
  note: `wezterm::PaneTextSource` defines an item `get_text_with_cx`, perhaps you need to implement it
```

## Root cause hypothesis
Asupersync migration landed `_with_cx` **callers** in some files without landing `_with_cx` **implementations** on the corresponding traits/structs, specifically under the default feature set (where the legacy impls are gated off but the new impls aren't gated on).

## Scope
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs (record_event_with_cx)
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs (get_text_with_cx + PaneTextSource trait alignment)
- 61 errors total; likely same pattern elsewhere

## Fix direction
1. Run `cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features 2>&1 | grep 'error\[\|error:' > /tmp/errors.txt` to enumerate all 61.
2. Group by method name; for each missing `*_with_cx` method:
   - If the non-cx variant exists, add a default impl or route `with_cx` through it (respects cancel semantics).
   - If neither exists, revert the caller to the non-cx variant with a TODO referencing ft-xbnl0.2 epic.
3. Verify with `cargo check` clean, then `cargo test --lib --no-default-features`.

## Priority
P0 — blocks swarm from running any tests under --no-default-features, which is the recommended lean test surface.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:29:56.725238Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680762Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680537Z","close_reason":"Added no-feature cx compatibility shims and ungated fallback *_with_cx adapters so frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features compiles again","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Depends on: ft-dzats (Phase 1) — easier with the test-tail removed first.

 {"id":"ft-6scm7","title":"[gui][i18n] font.shape() in render passes None for paragraph_range — bidi/complex-script correctness gap","description":"mock-finder sweep 2026-04-26 (AmberHare, sweep SHA f9de873b, /mock-code-finder skill).

Production gap: crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/render/mod.rs:835 calls font.shape(...) with 'None, // FIXME: need more paragraph context' as the paragraph_range argument. The FIXME has lived in this branch since the GUI extraction; without paragraph context the harfbuzz / freetype shaping path cannot resolve bidi (right-to-left embedding levels) or run-boundary-sensitive complex script clusters (Arabic medial vs final forms, Devanagari conjuncts, Mongolian, etc.) correctly. Result: visible mis-rendering of RTL and Indic scripts inside frankenterm-gui panes.

Citation: crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/render/mod.rs:835 — within the cluster shaping loop that iterates ShapedCluster items inside the screen-line render path (the call site is the inner font.shape(...) inside the screen_line render machinery; the FIXME is on the line passing the paragraph_range argument).

Suggested fix:
  1. Audit how the upstream wezterm-equivalent render path threads paragraph context (likely a Range<usize> over the logical line bytes, or a reference to a precomputed bidi paragraph) and re-establish that thread.
  2. Plumb a paragraph context through the cluster builder so the shape call receives Some(&paragraph) for the cluster's enclosing logical line.
  3. Add a render-level golden test (insta or similar) covering 1 RTL string ('السلام عليكم') and 1 Indic string ('नमस्ते') — failure mode here is silent mis-shaping, so a screenshot/text-attribute comparison is the only way to lock the fix.

Acceptance: the FIXME line in render/mod.rs:835 no longer passes None; companion render-golden test for at least one RTL + one Indic sample. Severity rationale (P2 not P3): correctness regression for any pane that renders non-Latin script — visible to international users.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"codex-greyfalcon","created_at":"2026-04-26T23:27:46.754993Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-27T18:57:26.194631Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T18:57:26.194395Z","close_reason":"Shipped ab11211d: render shaping now uses paragraph text/ranges for clusters, with RTL+Indic range regression coverage.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Verified clean via rch on /tmp/ft-cc6-target: 'cargo check --workspace' clean; 'cargo check -p frankenterm-core' clean; 'cargo check -p frankenterm-core-tantivy' clean; 'cargo check -p frankenterm-core --tests --features recorder-lexical' clean modulo cc7's WIP min_supported field on codec::GetCodecVersionResponse (kuxho.3 lane, out of scope, verified zero non-codec errors after extraction). -{"id":"ft-l6sq5","title":"[mcp-v1-002] register wa.workflow_status as MCP tool + add filter-required test (deferred from ft-zaqi8 matrix)","description":"## Surfaced by
ft-zaqi8 (MCP spec coverage matrix): clause MCP-V1-002 in
docs/mcp-api-spec.md:182 reads:

> Note: At least one of \`execution_id\`, \`pane_id\`, or \`active\`
> must be provided.

…on \`wa.workflow_status\`. The matrix marked this DEFERRED because
\`wa.workflow_status\` is not yet registered as an MCP tool — \`grep
'wa.workflow_status' crates/frankenterm-core/src/\` returns zero
matches. The clause is in the spec but the surface to enforce it
doesn't exist.

## Acceptance
1. Register \`wa.workflow_status\` as an MCP tool in the manifest +
   handler dispatch.
2. Add a test that calls it with all three filters absent and
   asserts an FT-MCP-0001 envelope rejection.
3. Annotate the new test with \`// MCP-V1-002\`.
4. Update docs/mcp-api-spec-coverage.md row MCP-V1-002 to
   Status=TESTED.
5. \`cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test conformance_mcp_coverage\`
   stays green.

## Out of scope
- Broader workflow_status semantics — this bead is just about the
  filter-required guard.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-28T10:14:29.284966Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T10:14:29.284966Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","follow-up","mcp"]}

 {"id":"ft-l6v1r","title":"[mor:distributed-systems] missed PaneDestroyed leaks tier/state maps across the swarm","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier` holds `RwLock<HashMap<u64, PaneState>>`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/pane_tiers.rs:238). Entries are added in
`register_pane` and removed in `unregister_pane`. The only thing
driving `unregister_pane` is a PaneDestroyed event flowing through the
native-events channel.

That channel has three failure modes documented in
`dispatch_event_with_timeout_with_cx`:
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Sent` — happy path
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Backpressure` — timeout expired, event dropped
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Closed` — channel gone

Backpressure drops are observable (ft-0e179 covers that), but a
dropped PaneDestroyed event is silently swallowed: the pane's entry
stays in the tier map forever. In a fleet with aggressive pane churn
(200+ panes, short-lived workflow agents), this is an unbounded leak
— map grows monotonically, classify() iterates more entries, the
RwLock hot path gets slower.

# Question

Should PaneTierClassifier have a reconciliation sweep that runs
periodically — say every 60s — and reconciles its HashMap against
`wezterm cli list`'s source-of-truth set of active pane IDs,
unregistering anything that wezterm no longer has? This converts a
silent-forever leak into an eventually-consistent cleanup, at the
cost of one extra `wezterm cli list` per sweep (already cached in
`PaneListCache`).

Same leak shape likely exists in other per-pane maps
(backpressure.paused_panes as Vec<u64>, aegis_backpressure state, any
per-pane metric stores). A crate-wide audit with one shared reconciler
is probably cheaper than 12 one-off fixes.

# CAP stance

Current design is "consistency by event delivery." On paper, the
event stream is authoritative. In practice, bounded queues mean
delivery is not guaranteed. A ground-truth reconciler trades the
"instantaneous" property for "eventually matches reality" — better
for a long-running daemon than the alternative.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:51.772093Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.358904Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788109Z","close_reason":"Verified in d13fe125","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed-systems","mor","pane-lifecycle"]}
 {"id":"ft-l9mxa","title":"DistributedHttpClient response-read path does not observe mid-flight cx-cancel","description":"# DistributedHttpClient mid-flight cx-cancel gap

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 verification work. When a caller fires
`cx.cancel_with(...)` WHILE a `DistributedHttpClient::get` is in flight (blocked
reading response bytes from a stalled server), the cancel does NOT propagate to
the HTTP client's read loop.

## Reproduction
The ft-xbnl0.2.4 test
`distributed::tests::distributed_http_client_mid_flight_cancel_does_not_hang`
exercises this. Observed behavior:

    elapsed = 10.02s
    result = Err("deadline has elapsed at Time(10000000041ns)")

The elapsed = ~10s matches the test's DEFENSIVE outer timeout ceiling
(`crate::runtime_compat::timeout(10s, client.get(...))`), not the 50 ms
cx-cancel fire. The cancel went through (cx.is_cancel_requested() would return
true) but the HTTP client's await points didn't observe it.

## Relation to other findings
- **Tick 328** documented that `timeout_with_cx` observes cx *budget deadline*
  but not direct cx *cancel*. This is the same shape of gap at a different
  layer — the HTTP client wraps asupersync::http::h1 whose await points
  don't poll cx.
- **ft-kfkyi** (closed tick 351) was a distinct concern (3xx redirect
  exfil). This finding is lower-severity: not a security issue, just an
  operational-ergonomics gap.

## Impact
- Hung distributed RPC calls will eventually time out via internal limits
  (if any configured) but won't observe operator-initiated cancel.
- Agent workflows that fire cx.cancel to abort a slow peer call will wait
  the full internal-timeout window before the call returns.

## Fix approach (candidate paths)
1. Extend `DistributedHttpClient::get/post` to race the inner future against
   a cx-cancel future (tokio::select! or similar race).
2. Patch asupersync::http::h1 to thread cx through its await points and
   honor cancel at read/write boundaries. Bigger scope; upstream change.
3. Wrap the DistributedHttpClient inner calls in `timeout_with_cx` —
   but per tick 328, that only observes budget, not cancel. So this
   doesn't help unless the caller sets a short budget.

Option (1) is the narrowest change; aligns with the cx-first migration
pattern and doesn't require upstream asupersync work.

## Verification
The existing tick-380 snapshot test is the flip trigger. When the fix lands:
1. Observed elapsed drops from ~10s to << 10s (cx-cancel observed mid-read).
2. `result` shape changes from the outer-timeout Err to the HTTP client's
   own cancel-shaped Err.
3. Update the snapshot assertions to require the faster behavior.

## References
- Finding: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 (commit 4d925607)
- Related: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5` out-of-scope carve-out
  for primitive-doc-only coverage (timeout_with_cx / sleep_with_cx — ticks
  328 / 331).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:43.612639Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342908Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342659Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 387 (commit 99640e41): DistributedHttpClient::{get,post} now race inner call against cx-cancel watcher (50ms poll). Mid-flight cancel surfaces as Ok(Err(Cancelled)) in ~70ms. Tick-380 snapshot test tightened to assert the new fast-cancel behavior (elapsed < 1s, exact Cancelled variant).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","cx-cancel","distributed","http"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-l9mxa","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:58.856854Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
